text: The prefrontal cortex does not generate attention so much as govern it. Neuroscientist Earl Miller's work at MIT revealed that the PFC sends top-down signals that bias competition among sensory neurons, amplifying representations of task-relevant stimuli while suppressing irrelevant ones. Think of it less as a spotlight operator and more as a casting director: it decides which neural actors get stage time. When PFC function is compromised — by sleep deprivation, stress, or aging — that editorial authority weakens, and every distraction wins its audition. The cortex is not broken; its gatekeeping simply goes offline.
question: According to the passage, what happens to attentional control when PFC function is compromised?

options.text: The PFC generates stronger signals to compensate for the disruption
Sensory neurons stop responding to external stimuli altogether
The editorial authority that suppresses distractions weakens
Working memory capacity increases to offset the loss
